prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
The attention mechanism in transformer models is a way for the model to focus on different parts of the input when processing each word. Think of it like this: when you're reading a sentence, you don't just look at each word in isolation. Instead, you pay attention to the words around it to understand the full context.

In a transformer model, each word is represented as a vector (a list of numbers). The attention mechanism calculates a score, called an "attention score," between every pair of words in the input. These scores determine how much each word should attend to every other word.

The scores are calculated using a query, key, and value for each word. The query and key are vectors that interact to produce the attention score, while the value is used to create the output representation. The attention scores are then used to weight the values, so the model can focus on the most relevant words.

This allows the model to capture complex relationships between words, even if they are far apart in the input. By learning which words to attend to, the transformer can better understand the context and generate more accurate outputs.
